title: The Overlander
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s a trade you all know wellן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s bringing cattle overן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll tell you all about the time
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When I became a drover.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I made up my mind to try the spec,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the Clarence I did wander,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bought a mob of duffers there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To begin as an overlander.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Chorus

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Pass the wine cup round, my boys;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Donן¿½t let the bottle stand there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  For to-night weן¿½ll drink the health
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Of every overlander.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Next morning counted the cattle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saw the outfit ready to start,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saw all the lads well mounted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And their swags put in a cart.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All kinds of men I had
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From France, Germany, and Flanders;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lawyers, doctors, good and bad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the mob of overlanders.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Next morning I set out
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the grass was green and young;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they swore theyן¿½d break my snout
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I did not move along.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I said, ן¿½Youן¿½re very hard;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Take care, donן¿½t raise my dander,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Iן¿½m a regular knowing card,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Queensland overlander.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis true we pay no license,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And our run is rather large;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis not often they can catch us,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So they cannot make a charge.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They think we live on store beef,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But no, Iן¿½m not a gander;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When a good fat stranger joins the mob,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Heן¿½ll do,ן¿½ says the overlander.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One day a squatter rode up.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Says he, ן¿½Youן¿½re on my run;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ve got two boys as witnesses.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Consider your stock in pound.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I tried to coax, then bounce him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But my tin I had to squander,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he put threepence a head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the mob of the overlander.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The pretty girls in Brisbane
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were hanging out their duds.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wished to have a chat with them,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So steered straight for the tubs.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some dirty urchins saw me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And soon they raised my dander,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crying, ן¿½Mother, quick! take in the clothes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here comes an overlander!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In town we drain the wine cup,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And go to see the play,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And never think to be hard up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For how to pass the day.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each has a sweetheart there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dressed out in all her grandeurן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dark eyes and jet black flowing hair.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sheן¿½s a plum,ן¿½ says the overlander.
meaning:
Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "The Overlander"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
